planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in moist to wet, well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; Fanal requires consistently moist soil — it will not tolerate prolonged drought, and in zone 9 summer heat the crown should be mulched deeply and irrigation provided during dry periods.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; Fanal is one of the most widely grown and celebrated astilbe cultivars — a Georg Arends hybrid producing deep blood-red to garnet-red plume-like flower spikes in early summer above a mound of attractive, bronzy-green, finely cut compound foliage. The dark red flower color of Fanal is among the deepest and most saturated in the astilbe genus — a vivid crimson-red that provides maximum color impact in the shaded border when paired with white, pale pink, or silver-foliage companions. The dark, bronzy-tinted foliage is ornamental even when the plant is not in bloom. Astilbes are among the finest shade-garden perennials for moist, humus-rich soils — they thrive in conditions that challenge most plants: deep shade, wet spots near streams and ponds, and the damp cool environments under trees. In zone 9, astilbes require careful siting in deep shade with consistently moist soil and good organic matter to succeed through the summer heat — they are not as reliable in zone 9 as in zone 5–7. Mulch heavily to keep the crown cool and moist. The dried flower plumes remain attractive for weeks after bloom and provide winter garden interest. Divide every 3–4 years in early spring to maintain vigor.

common_name: Aureola Hakone Grass (Golden Japanese Forest Grass)

planting.note: Plant container divisions in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; Aureola's golden-yellow variegation fades to green in deep shade — partial shade with 2–4 hours of dappled light produces the brightest color while preventing leaf scorch.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; Aureola is the most widely grown and celebrated variegated selection of Hakonechloa macra — Japanese forest grass — a graceful, slow-spreading, clump-forming grass producing cascading, arching blades variegated with creamy yellow to bright golden-yellow longitudinal stripes on a green background, creating one of the most luminous and elegant shade-garden ground covers available. The soft, cascading habit of the blades, with each tuft flowing gently in even the slightest breeze, creates a fluid, calming motion in the garden that no other shade grass rivals. Aureola was named Perennial Plant of the Year 2009 and consistently ranks among the most highly regarded shade plants in American horticulture. The golden variegation brightens shaded areas where color is difficult to achieve — planted with dark hostas, dark-leafed heucheras, or deep green ferns, Aureola creates outstanding luminous contrast. In zone 9, it grows as a deciduous to semi-evergreen perennial that may struggle in the hottest, driest summer conditions; provide consistent moisture, deep shade, and organic mulch. The blades turn rich orange-red in fall, adding seasonal interest. Slow to establish but long-lived once mature.
sources: Perennial Plant Association — Perennial Plant of the Year 2009

planting.note: Plant container trees in spring after danger of frost has passed in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral (pH 6.0–7.0), fertile soil in full sun; navel oranges are more cold-sensitive than mandarins and satsumas — protect from freezes below 28°F with frost cloth or temporary structures.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral (pH 6.0–7.0), fertile soil in full sun; the Washington Navel Orange is the world's most widely grown orange cultivar and the standard against which other eating oranges are judged — a seedless, easy-to-peel, richly flavored orange with the characteristic small secondary undeveloped fruit at the blossom end that gives the navel its name. The sweet, complex, aromatic flavor and seedless, easy-peeling segments make navel orange the premier fresh-eating orange for home orchards. The tree is vigorous and productive, bearing the main crop from November through April in zone 9 — the cool winter months concentrate sugars and develop the characteristic navel flavor. Navel oranges require warm, frost-free conditions and are best suited to zone 9–11; in inland zone 9 where winter freezes below 28°F occasionally occur, trees should be planted in thermally protected locations such as south-facing walls or sheltered courtyards, and protected with frost cloth during cold events. Fertilize with citrus-specific fertilizer (high nitrogen, micronutrient-balanced) in February, May, and September in zone 9. Water consistently but never allow waterlogging — citrus is highly susceptible to Phytophthora root rot in saturated soils. Thin to remove crossing branches but avoid heavy pruning.

common_name: Eureka Lemon (Standard Lemon Tree, Four-Season Lemon)

planting.note: Plant container trees in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral (pH 6.0–7.0), fertile soil in full sun; Eureka lemon is among the most cold-sensitive of commonly grown citrus — protect from temperatures below 28°F, which can cause significant damage to the tree.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral (pH 6.0–7.0), fertile soil in full sun; Eureka is the standard lemon cultivar in American commercial production and one of the most widely grown home-orchard lemons in zone 9–11 — a vigorous, nearly thornless tree that is truly everbearing, producing lemons year-round with peak crops in winter and spring. The classic medium-to-large, yellow lemons have thin, smooth skin, high juice content, classic lemon acidity, and excellent fragrance — the gold standard of lemon flavor for culinary use. The everbearing production habit means there is nearly always some fruit on the tree in zone 9, even if the heaviest crops come in winter and early spring. The tree reaches 8–15 feet with glossy, aromatic leaves and fragrant white flowers that bloom repeatedly throughout the year. Eureka is slightly more cold-sensitive than Lisbon lemon and significantly more cold-sensitive than mandarins or kumquats; temperatures below 28°F for more than 4 hours can cause fruit and leaf damage. In cooler parts of zone 9, Lisbon lemon (more cold-tolerant) or Meyer lemon (much more cold-tolerant) may be better choices. Fertilize with a citrus-specific balanced fertilizer three times annually; iron and zinc deficiencies are common in alkaline soils and should be corrected with chelated micronutrient sprays.

planting.note: Plant container shrubs in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; Variegata pittosporum is highly salt-tolerant and is an excellent choice for coastal landscapes within a mile of the Gulf or Atlantic coast.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; Variegated pittosporum is one of the most reliable and widely planted foundation and landscape shrubs for zone 8–11 coastal and Gulf Coast gardens — a compact, rounded, evergreen shrub producing distinctive, leathery, spatula-shaped leaves in dark green irregularly edged and streaked with creamy white to gray-green variegation. The variegated foliage provides year-round visual interest and brightness in both full sun and partial shade conditions. In spring, the shrub produces small, creamy-white, intensely orange-blossom fragrant flowers — the fragrance is remarkably strong for such small flowers and perfumes the surrounding garden. Variegated pittosporum is among the most salt-tolerant and wind-tolerant of common landscape shrubs, making it exceptional for coastal exposures where most shrubs struggle with salt spray and marine wind. The compact, naturally mounding habit stays 3–5 feet tall and wide with minimal pruning, though it can be pruned to control size immediately after spring bloom. Extremely drought-tolerant once established; tolerates clay soil, alkaline conditions, and reflected heat from paving. Avoid wet, poorly drained sites where Phytophthora root rot can be a problem. Essentially pest-free in zone 9. The standard green species Pittosporum tobira grows larger and is also widely planted in Gulf Coast landscapes.
